items.question: Is it worth replacing a Land Rover engine?
items.answer: It depends entirely on vehicle value versus replacement cost. A Range Rover Sport or Defender L663 will almost always justify replacement — the car is worth far more than the engine. A Freelander 2 or early Discovery Sport often won't; replacement cost can exceed what the car is worth outright. Use our diagnostic calculator above to get a personalised verdict.

items.question: Which Land Rover engine should I avoid?
items.answer: The 2.7 TDV6 (276DT) and the 3.0 TDV6/SDV6 (306DT) carry the highest catastrophic-failure risk in the range — both can suffer bottom-end failures that leave little to rebuild. The 4.4 SDV8 and post-2019 Ingenium units are considerably more dependable. See our Engine Rankings for the full breakdown.

items.question: Is the Ingenium engine reliable?
items.answer: The petrol Ingenium (204PT) has a noticeably better reliability record than the diesel (204DTD), which suffered timing chain tensioner issues on pre-2019 builds. JLR revised the design from 2019 onward, and those engines carry meaningfully lower risk. Check our Ingenium Knowledge Centre for full details.

items.question: Discovery 4 vs Discovery 5 — which is more reliable?
items.answer: The Discovery 5 benefits from the revised, more dependable Ingenium engines and a lighter aluminium platform. The Discovery 4 is more readily available on the SDV6, which is powerful but carries real crankshaft bearing risk at higher mileages — service history matters more than the badge.

items.question: How do I know if my Land Rover is worth repairing?
items.answer: Compare your realistic vehicle value against the full replacement quote, not just the repair estimate. As a rule, if replacement cost sits above roughly 60% of what the car is worth, it's worth getting a scrap valuation before committing. Use our Ownership Decision Tool for a personalised assessment.

items.question: Which is the safest Range Rover to buy used?
items.answer: The L405 (2012–2021) offers the widest margin between typical resale value and replacement cost of any generation, which is why it tops our safest-to-buy ranking. The newer L460 is even safer on paper, but at a significantly higher entry price.
